title: Pheromone Trail Maintenance
desc: The chemical trails that guide Mandiblors. Refresh them or the hive gets lost.

steps.text:
The pheromone trails are invisible highways — chemical signals painted on tunnel walls that tell every Danian where to go, what's ahead, and how to get home. Without them, Mount Pillar's labyrinthine tunnels would be unnavigable. The trails fade over time and need regular refreshing.

The trail crew carries ceramic pots of synthesized pheromone — each pot a different signal. Blue pot: "safe passage." Red pot: "hazard ahead." Gold pot: "food source." Green pot: "Queen's way — priority traffic."

The crew lead dips a brush into the blue pot and paints a stripe along the tunnel wall. "Match the existing trail exactly. Same height, same width, same intervals. If you deviate, you create a false branch that sends workers into dead ends."

She hands you a brush and a blue pot. "Corridor nine-south. Six hundred feet. Don't skip the junction markers."

options.label: Paint the safe-passage trail — steady and precise
Check the junction markers first — those are critical

steps.text:
You paint with the precision of someone who understands that a brushstroke in the wrong place could send fifty Mandiblors into a maintenance shaft.

The trail runs at shoulder height — a consistent stripe of pheromone that reads as "safe, keep going, nothing to worry about" to any Danian who passes. The old trail is faded but visible, and you paint directly over it, refreshing the signal.

The work is meditative. Brush dip, stroke, move forward. The pheromone smells faintly of pine resin — a chemical coincidence that makes the work pleasant. Six hundred feet of corridor, one arm-length at a time.

At the three-hundred-foot mark, you find something odd. The old trail forks — a faded split where someone painted two trails diverging. But the tunnel doesn't fork here. There's only one path. Someone created a phantom junction.

You paint over the false fork, restoring the single trail. Then you examine the wall where the false trail pointed. There's a hairline crack — and behind it, the hollow sound of an empty space. A sealed chamber, unmarked on any map.

You mark it for the survey team and continue painting. The corridor is refreshed, the false fork erased. Whatever's behind that wall, it's been there long enough for the trail that pointed to it to fade. Somebody, at some point, wanted people to find that chamber. Or wanted to remember where it was.

You finish the corridor and report the anomaly. The crew lead adds it to the survey queue. In Mount Pillar, the walls always have secrets.

options.label: Finish the trail and file the anomaly

steps.text:
Junction markers are the most critical pheromone signals — they tell Danians which fork to take at every intersection. A wrong junction marker can redirect an entire shift of workers to the wrong level.

You check each junction in corridor nine-south. There are seven. Five are clean — the markers are faded but readable. The sixth is smudged, likely from a passing cart that brushed the wall. You repaint it with the gold pot — this junction leads to the fungal gardens.

The seventh junction is wrong. The marker says "safe passage" — blue — but the tunnel it points to is a dead-end maintenance shaft. The correct marker should be red for hazard. Someone painted over the red with blue.

Accident? Possibly. A tired trail painter grabbing the wrong pot. But the blue is fresh — more recent than the surrounding trail work. Someone deliberately changed this marker within the last few days.

You repaint it red and report the change. The crew lead takes it seriously. "Deliberate marker changes are sabotage. A blue marker on a dead-end shaft could trap workers during an emergency evacuation."

She dispatches a Mandiblor to review recent trail crew logs — who painted this section last, what pots they carried, whether the change matches their work order.

You finish the remaining junctions. All correct, all refreshed. The trails are solid. But someone changed a marker, and in the hive, nothing is done without purpose.

options.label: Report everything and let the investigation proceed
